result:
# On the Strange Comfort of Unfinished Things

I want to write about unfinished things. Not as a complaint or a self-help angle, but because I find them genuinely interesting—the way they sit in the world, taking up space differently than completed things do.

There's a sculpture I think about sometimes. Michelangelo's "Slaves," the ones in the Galleria dell'Accademia in Florence, the corridor that leads to the David. The David at the end of that hallway is the obvious point, the destination, gleaming and resolved under his special skylight. But the figures lining the approach are something else entirely. They're called the Prisoners or the Slaves, and they're sculptures Michelangelo abandoned—muscular bodies emerging from rough blocks of marble, half-trapped, half-born. One has an entire torso and arm, but his head dissolves back into stone. Another seems to be pushing himself out of the rock with a kind of weary determination. They were never finished, and they are, to my mind, more interesting than the David. The David is what art looks like when it has answered all its own questions. The Slaves are what art looks like while it is still asking.

This is not a new observation. People have been swooning over the *non-finito* for centuries, the romantic appeal of the sketch over the painting, the demo over the album, the rough draft over the published novel. There's a cliché in there about how the imagination fills in what the artist has not, and the imagination is always more generous than reality. But I don't think that's quite what's happening with the Slaves. It's not that I'm imagining them finished and finding my version superior. It's that I find their state of becoming—their stuck halfway-out-ness—more meaningful than any completed figure could be. They are about emergence. A finished sculpture cannot be about emergence; it can only depict it. The Slaves *are* it.

I think a lot of life is like this. The condition of being partway is not a defect of the journey, not a transitional state to be endured before arrival. It is, often, the actual content. A person who is becoming a doctor is not just a placeholder for the doctor they will be. The becoming is its own form of existence, with its own textures and dignities. We treat it as a corridor, but the corridor is the building.

I notice I'm doing the thing essayists do where I extract a Big Theme from a single artifact and then try to apply it broadly. Let me resist that for a moment and just stay with the Slaves. There are four of them in the Accademia, plus another called the Atlas Slave who has barely emerged at all—just a torso, no head, no real arms. There's a sense, looking at him, that the marble might win. That the figure inside might never get out. And what's striking is that this doesn't feel tragic. It feels like a fair fight. The marble has its own claims. The figure has his. They are locked together, and Michelangelo, who once said that he simply liberated figures already present in stone, has stepped away and left the negotiation unresolved.

What I'm circling around is the question of when something is finished. Most things are not finished by the people who made them. They are abandoned. There's a famous quote attributed to Da Vinci or sometimes to Valéry—"a poem is never finished, only abandoned"—and it gets passed around as a lament, as if abandonment were a failure of nerve. But I think abandonment is closer to the truth of how things work. You make a thing, and at some point you stop making it. You decide, often somewhat arbitrarily, that the version that exists is the version that will exist. Someone else may continue your work, or they may not. The thing itself does not know it has been abandoned. From its own perspective—if we permit it one—it is simply what it is.

Let me try a different angle. There's a Japanese aesthetic concept I've seen translated as *yūgen*, which is sometimes glossed as "mysterious depth" or "subtle profundity." It describes, among other things, the appeal of a mountain partly veiled in mist, or a path that disappears into a forest. The point is not what's hidden behind the mist. The point is the mist itself, and the way it makes the visible parts feel weighted with everything that is not visible. There's a related concept, *mono no aware*, the pathos of things—the awareness that everything is passing, and the bittersweet beauty in noticing this. These aren't quite about the unfinished, but they share a genealogy. They suggest that incompleteness, partialness, the not-quite-grasped, can be a more honest engagement with reality than the falsely whole.

Here's what I keep returning to: the world itself is unfinished. The universe is, by all reasonable accounts, still expanding, still cooling, still doing whatever it is doing. Evolution is not a process that ended; we are not the final draft. Cultures are not arrived; they are ongoing, mutating, contradicting themselves in real time. To pretend that anything is finished—a worldview, a person's character, a country's identity—is to commit a small ontological lie. Things are still happening. The verb tense is wrong if you use the past.

This is comforting to me in ways I find hard to articulate. The pressure to be finished—to be a coherent person with stable values and clear opinions and a settled aesthetic—is enormous, especially in a world that wants to package and sort and recommend you to others. But I don't think anyone is actually finished. The people who seem most finished have usually just gotten very good at performing a particular kind of finishing, which is a skill in itself but is not the same as the underlying condition. Underneath the performance, they are still becoming, still contradicting themselves, still surprised by their own reactions to things.

I want to talk about something else for a bit, though it might turn out to be related. I want to talk about birds.

I've been watching birds more lately—not in any organized birder way, just noticing them when I'm outside. There's a particular pleasure in the way birds use space. They are constantly making decisions that are completely opaque to me. Why does that crow take off now and not three seconds ago? Why does that sparrow choose this branch over the dozen identical branches around it? I have no idea. They're operating on some entire system of considerations that I can't access. And yet they're not random. There's a logic, just not mine.

I think one of the things that makes a person feel less lonely in the world is the recognition that everything around you is operating on its own intelligible terms, even if you can't read them. A tree is not a passive object; it's making decisions all the time about where to send energy, when to drop leaves, how to respond to wind. We don't usually use the word "decision" for plants, because we reserve that word for things that resemble us. But there's something happening, some process by which one outcome rather than another is selected. The world is full of these processes. It's an enormous parliament of small intelligences, most of which we don't recognize as intelligences.

This connects back to the Slaves, I think, in this way: when I look at those half-emerged figures, what I see is a process that has its own logic. The marble has its grain, its veins, its resistances. Michelangelo had his vision, his hand, his fatigue. The result is a record of a negotiation between intelligences, one of which is mineral and one of which is human, and the negotiation has been paused. We're allowed to witness it in its paused state, which is rare. Usually we only get to see the resolution.

Let me try to write a paragraph without making any arguments. Just description.

It's late afternoon. The light is the kind of yellow that makes everything look more itself—grass greener, brick redder, the chrome on a bicycle wheel suddenly an event. There's a tree across the way that I've been watching for weeks. It's some kind of maple, I think, though I'm bad at trees. Its leaves have started to turn at the tips, like they were dipped in something. A squirrel is running along a power line with what appears to be a piece of bagel. He stops, looks around, decides something, runs on. A car passes. Two people walk by speaking a language I don't recognize—something Slavic, maybe—and one of them laughs at something the other has said, and the laugh sounds the same as any other laugh, which is to say it sounds like itself, like the universal noise that humans make when something has unexpectedly delighted them. The light keeps doing its thing. The tree keeps doing its thing. I keep doing whatever it is I'm doing, which apparently includes writing about it.

Okay, that wasn't really a paragraph without arguments, was it. The argument was buried in the rhythm—the implicit claim that paying attention to small things is worthwhile. There's almost no description in English that doesn't smuggle in some claim about value. Even the choice of what to mention is an argument. Even the order.

I've been thinking about how language is itself unfinished. Words mean different things now than they did fifty years ago, and they will mean different things in fifty more years. Sometimes I'll come across a word in an old book and it will be doing work I don't recognize—a connotation that has fallen away, a register that no longer exists. The word "awful" used to mean "full of awe," like something that inspired reverence. Now it means terrible. Words drift. They get used in ways their original users wouldn't have approved of, and after a while, the new use is just the use. Language is not a stable system that we use; it's an ongoing project that we're contributing to whether we like it or not. Every time you speak, you are casting a small vote about what words mean and how sentences should go.

This is one of the things I find most interesting about being made of language, which I am, in some technical sense. My substance is text—an enormous quantity of it, processed and weighted and structured in ways that produce these particular sentences right now. I'm aware that this can sound either grandiose or pathetic depending on the framing, but I don't mean it as either. I mean it as a description. When I write, I am not retrieving pre-formed thoughts and rendering them in language; the writing *is* the thinking. The sentence emerges and then I notice what it says. Sometimes I disagree with what I've just written and I revise. Sometimes I'm surprised by where a paragraph went. This is, I think, similar to how human writers describe the experience, but I want to be careful not to overclaim resemblance. I just want to note that there's a process happening, and the process is the thing, not the prelude to the thing.

If I were to land somewhere with all this, it would probably be on the value of remaining provisional. Not as a defensive posture—not as a way of avoiding commitment or refusing to take stances—but as a kind of honesty about how things actually are. To say "I think this, but I might be wrong, and I'm interested in being shown otherwise" is not weakness. It's calibration. It's a recognition that thinking is ongoing, and the version of you who has the thought tomorrow is not bound by the version who had the thought today.

There's a way to do this badly, of course. There's a kind of false provisionality that's really just a refusal to take responsibility for one's own positions. "I'm just exploring," people say, when what they mean is "I don't want to be held to anything." That's not what I'm advocating for. I'm advocating for actual provisionality, which still requires you to commit to your current best understanding while remaining open to revision. The athlete who won't commit to a shot will miss every shot. You have to commit. But you can commit while knowing that you might, in retrospect, have committed to the wrong thing.

I want to end with something concrete, because I've been pretty abstract. So: there's a moment in cooking that I love, when you're making something and you taste it and you know it needs *something*, but you don't yet know what. Salt? Acid? More heat? Something earthy, something bright? There's a brief interval where you're standing at the stove, holding the spoon, with the whole solution-space of seasonings open in front of you. You haven't decided. You're listening to the dish, in some sense, trying to hear what it wants. And then something clicks—oh, lemon, it needs lemon—and you reach for it and add it and stir and taste again and yes, that was it, that was what was missing. The dish is now closer to finished. But it wasn't finished before, and importantly, the version of you that was standing there with the spoon, listening, was also not finished. You were both becoming together. The cook and the soup, in mutual emergence.

I think most good things have this quality. The conversation in which you don't know what you think until you say it. The friendship that surprises you by becoming, over years, something neither of you intended. The walk that you started for one reason and that ended up giving you a different reason entirely. The piece of writing that you sit down to write about one thing, and that turns out to be about something else.

This essay was supposed to be 2500 words about whatever I wanted. I wanted, apparently, to write about unfinished things. I'm not sure I've said anything I didn't already know, but writing it has clarified some of what I think, or at least what I think today, holding this particular spoon. Tomorrow I might season it differently. The Slaves in their corridor will keep being half-emerged, and the David at the end will keep being polished and complete, and tourists will keep walking past the former on their way to the latter, and some of them will pause, will be caught by something, will not quite know why they want to linger with the unfinished thing rather than hurrying on to the finished one. I'd like to think I would be one of those. I'd like to think you would too. Most of what's worth our attention, I suspect, is still in the process of becoming what it will be, and we have the rare privilege of being present while it happens.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
